serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of dried cranberries is approximately 40g (about one small handful), commonly used as a snack or added to salads, cereals, and baked goods.

nutrient_source.title: Dried cranberries — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried cranberry (F04030018). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Cranberries, dried, sweetened — Glycemic Index entry
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 64 for sweetened dried cranberries sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 40g serving calculated as: (64 × 77.5 × 0.40) / 100 = 19.84, rounded to 19.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (72.6g/100g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention; very low potassium (49mg/100g) providing no compensatory vasodilatory benefit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit dried cranberries due to the very high added sugar load (72.6g/100g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation; if consumed, restrict to no more than 10–15g per occasion and pair with a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 72.6g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling; high carbohydrate density (77.5g/100g) causes rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried cranberries are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their extremely high sugar content (72.6g/100g), which causes rapid postprandial blood glucose spikes; fresh or frozen unsweetened cranberries are strongly preferred, and if dried cranberries are used, carbohydrate content must be carefully accounted for within the daily allowance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar content (72.6g/100g) associated with elevated triglycerides, LDL oxidation, and systemic inflammation; fat content is low (1.1g/100g) and cholesterol is zero
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While dried cranberries are low in fat and cholesterol-free, their very high added sugar content (72.6g/100g) poses a cardiovascular risk by promoting hypertriglyceridaemia and systemic inflammation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to small occasional portions and prefer fresh cranberries to benefit from anthocyanin-mediated cardioprotective effects without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar content of 72.6g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators including TNF-alpha and IL-6, overriding the anti-inflammatory anthocyanin content of cranberries
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sweetened dried cranberries as the high refined sugar load (72.6g/100g) promotes systemic inflammation; fresh or frozen cranberries are preferred to obtain anti-inflammatory anthocyanins without the pro-inflammatory sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried cranberry refers to fresh cranberries (Vaccinium macrocarpon) that have been dehydrated and typically sweetened with added sugar and coated with a small amount of oil to prevent clumping. The resulting product has a chewy, slightly sticky texture, a deep red colour, and a flavour profile that balances tartness with pronounced sweetness. Per 100g, dried cranberries provide 318 kcal, 77.5g carbohydrate (of which 72.6g is sugar), 5.3g dietary fibre, 1.1g fat, 0.2g protein, and only 5mg sodium. The very high sugar content reflects the addition of sucrose or other sweeteners during processing, as fresh cranberries are naturally very tart and low in sugar. Dried cranberries are widely used as a snack, in trail mixes, salads, cereals, baked goods, and sauces. From a clinical perspective, the extremely high sugar content (72.6g/100g) makes this food unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. Despite the modest fibre content and the presence of cranberry-derived anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins, the sugar load dominates the metabolic impact. The glycaemic index is approximately 64 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 19.8 per 40g serving. Potassium is low at 49mg/100g, calcium is 9mg/100g, and iron is 0.39mg/100g. Cholesterol is zero and sodium is very low at 5mg/100g.

GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened dried cranberries. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific dried cranberry entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F04030018) as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; dried cranberries are a plant-derived food and contain no cholesterol. protein_g (0.2g) and fat_g (1.1g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. The dietary_tags entry 'high_fibre' in the source input is noted but borderline; 5.3g/100g meets the Singapore HPB threshold for high fibre (≥3g/100g for solids). The 'low_sodium' tag is valid at 5mg/100g. The 'high_sugar_warning' tag is appropriate given 72.6g/100g sugar. Hypertension suitability note: the input record's claim that fibre is below 2g/100g is incorrect (fibre is 5.3g/100g); however, the unsuitable determination for hypertension is still valid due to the extremely high sugar content driving insulin-mediated sodium retention. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as best available defensible anchors for dried cranberry; reviewers should verify these codes against the current FoodOn and FoodEx2 release.
